questions.stem: How many convex regular polyhedra (Platonic solids) are there?
questions.explanation: There are exactly five convex regular polyhedra: the tetrahedron, cube, octahedron, dodecahedron, and icosahedron. Theaetetus first proved there are only five.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The Schlafli symbol $\{p, q\}$ of a convex regular polyhedron records which data?
options: The number of vertices and edges.
The faces are regular $p$-gons and $q$ of them meet at each vertex.
The number of faces and the number of solids.
questions.explanation: In the Schlafli symbol $\{p, q\}$, each face is a regular $p$-gon and $q$ faces meet at each vertex. For example the cube is $\{4, 3\}$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The inequality that limits the possible Schlafli symbols $\{p, q\}$ of a convex regular polyhedron is which of the following?
questions.explanation: The angles at a vertex must sum to less than $2\pi$, which simplifies to $(p - 2)(q - 2) < 4$. The only solutions with $p, q \ge 3$ are the five Platonic solids.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: How many vertices does a cube have?
questions.explanation: A cube has $8$ vertices. In the symmetry analysis, the symmetry group acts transitively on these $8$ vertices, with stabilizer of order $3$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: How many faces meet at each vertex of a regular tetrahedron?
questions.explanation: At each vertex of a tetrahedron, $3$ triangular faces meet, so the stabilizer of a vertex in the rotation group is cyclic of order $3$. Its Schlafli symbol is $\{3, 3\}$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: What is the order of the rotational (orientation preserving) symmetry group of a regular tetrahedron?
questions.explanation: By Orbit-Stabilizer, the rotation group has $|\text{vertices}| \times |\text{stabilizer}| = 4 \times 3 = 12$ elements. This group is isomorphic to the alternating group $A_4$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: What is the order of the rotational symmetry group of a cube?
questions.explanation: The rotation group of the cube has $8 \times 3 = 24$ elements (8 vertices, stabilizer order 3). It is isomorphic to the symmetric group $S_4$, acting on the four long diagonals.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: What is the order of the rotational symmetry group of a regular icosahedron?
questions.explanation: The icosahedron has $12$ vertices with $5$ faces meeting at each, so its rotation group has $12 \times 5 = 60$ elements. It is isomorphic to the alternating group $A_5$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The rotational symmetry group of a regular tetrahedron is isomorphic to which group?
questions.explanation: The rotations correspond to the even permutations of the four vertices, so the rotation group is isomorphic to $A_4$. The full symmetry group, including reflections, is $S_4$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The full symmetry group of a regular tetrahedron (including orientation reversing symmetries) is isomorphic to which group?
questions.explanation: The symmetry group permutes the four vertices and any vertex permutation is realised, so it is isomorphic to $S_4$, of order $24$. A symmetry fixing all four vertices is the identity, so the map to $S_4$ is injective.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The rotational symmetry group of a cube is isomorphic to which group?
questions.explanation: The rotation group of the cube is isomorphic to $S_4$, of order $24$, acting as all permutations of the four long diagonals of the cube.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The full symmetry group of a cube (including reflections) is isomorphic to which group?
questions.explanation: The full symmetry group of the cube is $S_4 \times C_2$, of order $48$. The $C_2$ factor is generated by the antipodal map $x \mapsto -x$, which commutes with every symmetry.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The rotational symmetry group of a regular dodecahedron is isomorphic to which group?
questions.explanation: The rotation group of the dodecahedron is isomorphic to $A_5$, of order $60$. It acts as even permutations of the five cubes that can be inscribed in the dodecahedron.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Which pair of Platonic solids are dual to one another and therefore share the same symmetry group?
questions.explanation: The cube and octahedron are dual: the vertices of one are the face centres of the other. Dual solids have identical symmetry groups. The dodecahedron and icosahedron form the other dual pair.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Which Platonic solid is self-dual, that is dual to another copy of itself?
questions.explanation: The tetrahedron is self-dual: the centres of its faces are the vertices of another tetrahedron. This is why its rotation group $A_4$ and full group $S_4$ describe a single solid.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: How many vertices does a regular dodecahedron have?
questions.explanation: A dodecahedron has $20$ vertices, with $3$ pentagonal faces meeting at each. Its Schlafli symbol is $\{5, 3\}$ and its rotation group has order $20 \times 3 = 60$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: How many faces meet at each vertex of a regular icosahedron?
questions.explanation: Five triangular faces meet at each vertex of an icosahedron, so the vertex stabilizer is cyclic of order $5$. Its Schlafli symbol is $\{3, 5\}$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The full symmetry group of a regular dodecahedron is isomorphic to which group, and has which order?
questions.explanation: The full symmetry group of the dodecahedron is $A_5 \times C_2$, of order $120$. The $C_2$ comes from the antipodal map. Note this is not $S_5$, even though both have order $120$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Which Schlafli symbol corresponds to the regular octahedron?
questions.explanation: The octahedron has triangular faces ($p = 3$) with $4$ meeting at each vertex ($q = 4$), so its Schlafli symbol is $\{3, 4\}$. Its dual, the cube, is $\{4, 3\}$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: In the proof classifying finite rotation groups, the orbit $\Omega_1$ giving the octahedron case has $6$ points with vertex stabilizers of order $4$. These $6$ points are the vertices of which solid?
questions.explanation: The $6$ points fixed by order-$4$ rotations are the vertices of a regular octahedron. The face midpoints ($\Omega_2$) then form the dual cube, which shares the same symmetry group.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The rotation group of a regular tetrahedron corresponds to which permutations of the four vertices?
questions.explanation: Orientation preserving symmetries of the tetrahedron act as even permutations of the vertices, so the rotation group is $A_4$. Reflections give the odd permutations, completing $S_4$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Why do the cube and octahedron have the same symmetry group?
options: Because they have the same number of vertices.
Because they are dual: the vertices of the octahedron sit at the face centres of the cube.
Because they have the same number of faces.
questions.explanation: The octahedron is the dual of the cube, with its vertices at the cube's face centres. Any symmetry of the cube permutes those face centres, so the two solids share their symmetry group, $S_4 \times C_2$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: An octahedron has $6$ vertices, and the rotation group fixing one vertex has order $4$. Using the Orbit-Stabilizer theorem, give the order of the full rotational symmetry group of the octahedron.
questions.explanation: The vertices form one orbit of size $6$, and each stabilizer has order $4$, so the rotation group has $6 \times 4 = 24$ elements (isomorphic to $S_4$).
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: A regular dodecahedron has $20$ vertices, with a rotation stabilizer of order $3$ at each vertex. Give the order of its rotational symmetry group.
questions.solution: By Orbit-Stabilizer the order is $20 \times 3$, which equals {{1}}.
questions.explanation: With $20$ vertices in one orbit and stabilizer order $3$, the rotation group has $20 \times 3 = 60$ elements, isomorphic to $A_5$.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: Test the Schlafli symbol $\{4, 3\}$ of the cube against the regularity inequality $(p-2)(q-2) < 4$. Compute the value of $(p-2)(q-2)$ for $\{4, 3\}$.
questions.solution: With $p = 4$ and $q = 3$, the product $(p-2)(q-2) = (2)(1)$, which equals {{1}}.
questions.explanation: $(4-2)(3-2) = 2 \times 1 = 2$, which is less than $4$, so $\{4, 3\}$ is a valid Platonic solid, the cube.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups

questions.stem: The full symmetry group of a regular icosahedron is $A_5 \times C_2$. State whether this group is isomorphic to the symmetric group $S_5$.
questions.solution: Both $A_5 \times C_2$ and $S_5$ have order $120$, but they are not isomorphic, so the icosahedral group {{1}} isomorphic to $S_5$.
questions.explanation: The full icosahedral group is $A_5 \times C_2$. It has a nontrivial centre (the antipodal map), whereas $S_5$ has trivial centre, so the two order-$120$ groups are not isomorphic.
questions.source: in the style of T. K. Carne, Geometry and Groups
